title: Destructors
description: The opposite of a constructor: code that runs automatically right before an object is destroyed, commonly used for cleanup.
objectives: Understand a destructor as code that runs automatically when an object is destroyed
Know when a destructor fires for a stack object vs a heap object
Understand why destructors matter for cleaning up heap memory

sections.text: Constructors covered code that runs automatically when an object is created. A destructor is the mirror image: code that runs automatically right before an object is destroyed.
A destructor is a special member function that runs automatically right before an object is destroyed, commonly used to release any resources the object was holding.

sections.title: What makes it a destructor
points: Its name is the class's name prefixed with ~ — ~Student
It has no return type, and takes no parameters at all
It runs automatically, right before the object goes away

sections.output:
Before
Student created
After
Student destroyed

sections.title: Tracing it
points: Student s1; runs the constructor immediately — "Student created"
main() reaches its closing brace — recall Stack Memory: s1's stack frame is about to be popped, so its destructor fires right before that happens — "Student destroyed"

sections.text: 4. For Heap Objects, delete Triggers It

sections.code:
Student *s2 = new Student(); // constructor runs

delete s2; // destructor runs HERE, then the memory is freed

sections.text: A heap-allocated object (recall new and delete) doesn't get automatic stack cleanup — its destructor only runs when delete is explicitly called on it. Forgetting to delete a heap object means its destructor never runs at all, on top of the memory leak already covered in delete.
5. Why Destructors Matter: Cleaning Up Heap Memory

This pattern — acquiring a resource in the constructor and releasing it in the destructor — means a Buffer object always cleans up after itself the moment it's destroyed, without the programmer needing to remember to call delete[] manually every time. This idea has a name, RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization), which becomes more relevant in advanced C++.

Unlike constructors, a class can have only one destructor — it takes no parameters, so there's no way to overload it (recall Function Overloading requires differing parameter lists). Every object of that class is destroyed using the exact same destructor.

sections.title: Checking out of a hotel room
sections.text: Checking into a hotel room (the constructor) sets things up — keys issued, room assigned. Checking out (the destructor) automatically triggers cleanup — keys returned, the room reset for the next guest — and it happens the moment you leave, without you personally having to reset the room yourself.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the ~ in a destructor's name — ~Student(), not Student() again (that would just be another constructor attempt).
Giving a destructor parameters — it can never take any, which is also why it can never be overloaded.
Assuming a heap object's destructor runs automatically — it only runs when delete is explicitly called.
Not releasing resources (like heap memory) acquired in the constructor — the destructor is exactly the place that cleanup belongs.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a destructor?
questions.answer: A special member function that runs automatically right before an object is destroyed, commonly used to release resources the object was holding.

questions.question: How is a destructor named, and what does its signature look like?
questions.answer: The class's name prefixed with ~, with no return type and no parameters, e.g. ~Student().

questions.question: When does a destructor run for a stack-allocated object versus a heap-allocated one?
questions.answer: For a stack object, it runs automatically when the object goes out of scope; for a heap object created with new, it only runs when delete is explicitly called on it.

questions.question: Can a class have multiple overloaded destructors?
questions.answer: No — a destructor takes no parameters, so there's nothing to differentiate overloads by; a class has exactly one destructor.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A destructor is a special member function, named as the class's name prefixed with ~, with no return type and no parameters, that runs automatically right before an object is destroyed. For a stack-allocated object, this happens automatically when it goes out of scope; for a heap-allocated object created with new, the destructor only runs when delete is explicitly called. Destructors are commonly used to release resources acquired in the constructor — like heap memory — ensuring an object always cleans up after itself, and unlike constructors, a class can have only one destructor, since it takes no parameters to differentiate overloads by.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Destructors
sections.text: Destructor = checking out of a hotel room. ~ClassName(), no params, runs automatically. Constructor acquires, destructor releases.

Before
